title: Why Next.js?
intro: We're using Next.js for our task manager. Swipe right on real advantages of Next.js, left on myths or misconceptions people spread about it.

teach.title: Next.js = React with Superpowers
teach.body: Next.js is a React framework that handles the boring stuff for you: routing, server-side rendering, API endpoints, and deployment. Instead of stitching together 10 libraries yourself, Next.js gives you a batteries-included setup.

teach.title: React vs Next.js = Engine vs Car
teach.body: React is a great engine, but you still need to build the car around it — routing, server logic, build tools. Next.js gives you the whole car, ready to drive. You can still pop the hood and customize, but you start with something that works.
Next.js gives us file-based routing, built-in API routes, server-side rendering, and easy deployment. That's exactly what our task manager needs to go from zero to production fast.

cards.content: Next.js uses file-based routing, so you create pages by adding files to a folder instead of configuring a router
cards.explanation: File-based routing is one of Next.js's biggest wins. Create a file, get a route. No router config to maintain. For our task manager, each page is just a file.

cards.content: Next.js is only worth using for large enterprise applications with huge teams
cards.explanation: Next.js scales down just as well as it scales up. It's perfect for solo projects and small apps. The structure it provides actually helps beginners stay organized.

cards.content: Next.js lets you write backend API endpoints in the same project, so you don't need a separate server
cards.explanation: API routes mean your frontend and backend live in one codebase. For a task manager, this simplifies everything — no CORS issues, no separate deploy.

cards.content: You need to be a React expert before you can touch Next.js
cards.explanation: You need to know React basics like components and state, but Next.js actually makes many things easier than raw React. You can learn both together.

cards.content: Server-side rendering in Next.js helps your pages load faster and improves SEO
cards.explanation: SSR means the server sends a fully rendered page to the browser. Users see content immediately instead of a blank screen. Search engines can index it too.

cards.content: Every single page in a Next.js app must use server-side rendering, no exceptions
cards.explanation: Next.js gives you choices: static generation, server-side rendering, or client-side rendering. You pick what fits each page. The dashboard can be client-rendered while the landing page is static.

cards.content: Next.js apps can be deployed easily to platforms like Vercel with zero configuration
cards.explanation: Vercel (the company behind Next.js) makes deployment dead simple. Push to Git, get a live URL. For a task manager project, this means you can ship fast.
